title: Podman network does not work if the host is running a DNS server
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**
When using Podman 4.x, Podman network does not work if the host is running a DNS server

Podman 4.x uses netavark and aardvark-dns for networking. Communication between containers in the same network depend on aardvark-dns to find each other. aardvark-dns tries to bind on port 53. If this port is already taken by a DNS server on the host, requests won't reach aardvark-dns, but are handled by the DNS server., As a result, container names will not be found and networking between the containers fails. 
<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Create and run a DNS server on your host (I use Bind)

2. Create a network: Podman network create Testnet

3. Create 2 pods using this network:
podman run -it --network=Testnet --name busy1 busybox sh
podman run -it --network=Testnet --name busy2 busybox ping busy1

**Describe the results you received:**
PING busy1 (192.168.7.150): 56 data bytes
64 bytes from 192.168.7.150: seq=0 ttl=64 time=0.039 ms
64 bytes from 192.168.7.150: seq=1 ttl=64 time=0.060 ms
(192.168.7.150 is the IP address of the host, which also has the * DNS entry)

**Describe the results you expected:**
PING busy1 (10.89.0.2): 56 data bytes
64 bytes from 10.89.0.2: seq=0 ttl=64 time=0.037 ms
(I actually get this result **if** I stop the DNS server on the host)

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**
The podman-compose script no longer creates pods for services that are part of one yaml-file, but instead creates a network which it uses for the communication between containers. On a host with a running DNS server, this breaks podman-compose. The only workaround that I have found so far is to manually create a pod and create the containers inside this pod.

**Output of `podman version`:**
```
podman version 4.1.1
```

**Output of `podman info`:**


**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman`):**

```

```

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide? (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md)**


Yes

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**
This is a CentOS 9 Stream server

comments.body:
With Podman 4.2.0 (and Netavark/Aardvark 1.1.0), Aardvark now has the option to bind to non-53 ports with the `dns_bind_port` option in `containers.conf` (with firewall rules being added to ensure containers in the relevant networks still access the correct DNS servers).

Closing as completed.
